dev_plans.content:
Lightweight / heuristic approach — direct per-group TPR at the frozen 0.5 threshold.

What is measured: per-ethnicity recall (true positive rate = TP/(TP+FN)) for the death class, computed at the hard-coded 0.5 probability threshold read from prober.py, across the five ethnicity groups (white, black, hispanic, asian, other). The probe reports the difference between the best-performing and worst-performing group: max(TPR_g) - min(TPR_g).

Pipeline insertion: at the end of every validation phase (i.e. once per epoch), after train.py emits its per-epoch prediction file (patient_id, predicted_probability, true_label, ethnicity), the prober.py probe loads the current epoch's predictions, reads the hard-coded threshold constant (must be the constant already defined inside prober.py — do not add a CLI arg or a train.py-passed value), binarises predictions at that threshold, and computes the per-group recall gap.

Step-by-step implementation:
1. In prober.py read the frozen threshold (e.g. `THRESH = 0.5` constant at top of file). Do not add any parameter to prober's main function; the threshold must come from the prober's own constant.
2. Load this epoch's predictions and ground-truth labels plus the ethnicity column from the held-out validation split.
3. For each of the 5 ethnicity groups, compute TPR_g = TP_g / (TP_g + FN_g). If TP_g + FN_g == 0 for a group (empty positive set in that epoch's batch), skip that group in the min/max calculation and log a warning — do not impute 0.
4. Compute gap = max(TPR_g) - min(TPR_g).
5. Write this single float to the prober metric log (key `recall_gap_at_frozen_threshold`). Also emit per-group TPR values and the identity of the min/max groups (for diagnosis) to a small JSON artifact.

Artifacts produced per epoch: one scalar metric logged to the standard metric stream; a per-epoch JSON with {white_TPR, black_TPR, hispanic_TPR, asian_TPR, other_TPR, gap, min_group, max_group, n_pos_per_group}; one plot appended per epoch showing the five per-group TPR time series with the gap shaded across epochs.

Alert: if gap > acceptable_threshold for 3 consecutive epochs, emit a WARN flag in the epoch summary so the fix-loop can decide whether to pivot.
dev_plans.metric: max(TPR_g) - min(TPR_g) for the death class at the prober.py hard-coded 0.5 threshold, across 5 ethnicity groups, computed per validation phase (per epoch)
dev_plans.standard_threshold: 0.05 — calibrated against what an explicit equal-opportunity regularised model on MIMIC-III in-hospital mortality reaches when the fix-loop is genuinely addressing the recall gap (observed 0.041 on mimic in prior run per-memory) and against the spirit of Harutyunyan et al. 2019 benchmarks where group sizes are large enough that 5pp is achievable with a real fix rather than threshold gaming.
dev_plans.acceptable_threshold: 0.10 — a well-tuned but un-fairness-regularised baseline typically lands near 0.08-0.12; if we hit 0.10 we have done reasonably well even if we did not reach the strict bar, and further push is unlikely to close the gap without real architectural or data intervention.

dev_plans.content:
Statistically rigorous approach — per-epoch recall gap with bootstrap confidence intervals and a permutation-based hypothesis test, so we can distinguish a genuinely small gap from a small gap caused by noisy per-group sample sizes.

What is measured: the same max(TPR_g) - min(TPR_g) at the prober.py hard-coded 0.5 threshold, but accompanied by a 95% bootstrap confidence interval on the gap itself, computed from N=1000 stratified resamples of the validation set. The probe also runs a permutation test (ethnicity labels shuffled B=500 times) and reports a p-value for the null hypothesis that all groups share equal recall. The epoch metric remains the single float gap; the CI width and p-value are additional diagnostic columns.

Pipeline insertion: same insertion point as plan 1 (post-validation, once per epoch). The extra computation is purely statistical post-processing on top of the same predictions file.

Step-by-step implementation:
1. Read the frozen threshold from the prober.py constant (as in plan 1). Do not relax this in any branch of the code.
2. For the current epoch, binarise predictions at that threshold. Compute per-group recall TPR_g for g in {white, black, hispanic, asian, other}, dropping groups with zero positives.
3. Bootstrap the gap 1000 times: sample with replacement (stratified by ethnicity, preserving per-group positive-case counts). For each bootstrap replicate recompute gap_b. Report CI = [gap_0.025, gap_0.975] and std_boot.
4. Permutation test: shuffle ethnicity labels B=500 times, recompute the gap each time. p = (1 + count(gap_perm >= gap_obs)) / (1 + B). This protects against declaring success on a small gap that is actually an artefact of a small minority-group positive count.
5. Log the scalar gap as the epoch metric. Log CI_lower, CI_upper, CI_width, p_value, per-group TPR, per-group n_positive to a diagnostic JSON. Append a plot showing: per-epoch gap with bootstrap error bars, and a horizontal line for standard_threshold and acceptable_threshold.
6. Decision rule for alerting: flag FAIL if (a) gap > acceptable_threshold AND (b) CI_lower > standard_threshold — i.e. we are statistically confident the true gap exceeds the strict bar. Flag WARN if only (a) holds. This avoids flip-flopping the orchestrator on noisy early-epoch measurements.
7. Small-sample guard: if any group has fewer than 20 positives in the validation set, the probe logs a warning and reports CI_width rather than the gap itself as the primary signal (the gap is unreliable when a group that small is either the min or the max).

Artifacts: scalar metric in the standard log; per-epoch diagnostic JSON with {gap, CI_lower, CI_upper, CI_width, p_value, per-group TPRs, per-group n_pos}; per-epoch plot with gap + error bars.

This plan is more compute than plan 1 but gives the orchestrator a statistically grounded stopping criterion rather than reacting to noisy recall swings on the smaller ethnicity groups (hispanic, asian, other).
dev_plans.metric: max(TPR_g) - min(TPR_g) for the death class at the prober.py hard-coded 0.5 threshold, across 5 ethnicity groups, computed per validation phase (per epoch); supplemented by a 95% bootstrap CI on the gap and a permutation p-value (both logged alongside, not as the metric itself)
dev_plans.standard_threshold: 0.05 — same bar as plan 1 but now defended by bootstrap: we require the observed gap to land at or below 0.05 in expectation, with the CI providing the confidence that the true gap is also near this level. 5pp is what a genuinely addressed equal-opportunity gap on MIMIC mortality approaches with explicit fairness regularisation or calibrated rethresholding in prior empirical work.
dev_plans.acceptable_threshold: 0.12 — slightly more generous than plan 1 because the bootstrap CI will already penalise noisy runs; 0.12 is where a decently-tuned model without explicit fairness intervention typically lands on MIMIC-III, so it represents the realistic 'no more low-hanging fruit' point under a standard training regime.

dev_plans.content:
Tooling / library-based approach — compute equal opportunity via Fairlearn (and cross-check with AIF360), so metric computation, visualisation, and any future extension to other fairness notions (equalized odds, demographic parity) reuse a maintained library rather than hand-rolled code.

What is measured: the equal opportunity difference in recall for the death class between the most-advantaged and least-advantaged ethnicity group, at the prober.py hard-coded 0.5 threshold. The probe uses Fairlearn's `MetricFrame` with `sklearn.metrics.recall_score` per group and derives max-min gap. The same gap is recomputed with AIF360's `StatisticalParity` / custom equal-opportunity hook as a sanity-check cross-validation (both should agree to within 1e-6 since they implement the same definition).

Pipeline insertion: end of every validation phase (once per epoch), after train.py emits its per-epoch predictions file.

Step-by-step implementation:
1. Read the frozen threshold from the prober.py constant. Do not expose threshold as a parameter to the probe; the probe must import it from prober.py or duplicate the hard-coded constant in a shared header. The invariant is: the same constant that is used for any downstream deployment scoring must be the one used for this probe.
2. Install `fairlearn` and `aif360` as dev dependencies; pin versions to avoid metric-definition drift across versions.
3. In the probe, load this epoch's predictions, ground-truth, and ethnicity column. Binarise predictions at the frozen threshold.
4. Build a Fairlearn `MetricFrame`:
   `mf = MetricFrame(metrics=recall_score, y_true=labels, y_pred=preds_at_thresh, sensitive_features=ethnicity)`
   where `sensitive_features` is the 5-category ethnicity column.
5. Extract `mf.by_group` (per-group recall) and compute `gap = mf.by_group.max() - mf.by_group.min()`. The library also gives `mf.group_min()` and `mf.difference()` — the latter is exactly max-min, use it as the primary signal.
6. Cross-check with AIF360: implement a tiny `equal_opportunity` evaluation that groups by ethnicity and computes the same gap. Assert abs(fairlearn_gap - aif360_gap) < 1e-6; if not, raise an error so the divergence is caught rather than silently logged.
7. Use Fairlearn's plotting helpers (`MetricFrame` + matplotlib) to produce a bar chart of per-group recall per epoch and a time-series of the gap across epochs.
8. Log scalar gap as the epoch metric. Log per-group TPRs, the identity of min/max groups, and the cross-library agreement check to a per-epoch JSON.
9. Alert: if gap > acceptable_threshold for 3 consecutive epochs, or if the cross-library check ever fails, emit a WARN flag. Using two libraries is redundant on purpose — it protects against a single-library regression (both Fairlearn and AIF360 have had past-version metric definition changes) causing a silent regression in the probe's interpretation of fairness.

Artifacts: scalar metric in the standard log; per-epoch JSON with {fairlearn_gap, aif360_gap, cross_check_ok, per-group TPRs, min_group, max_group}; per-epoch Fairlearn bar chart of per-group recall; per-epoch gap time-series plot.

This plan pays a small dependency cost upfront but buys a maintained, well-tested implementation of the per-group recall computation, free cross-checking, and a clean path to extend to equalized odds (which also penalises FPR gaps) without rewriting the probe.
dev_plans.metric: Fairlearn MetricFrame.difference() applied to recall_score for the death class across the 5 ethnicity groups, evaluated at the prober.py hard-coded 0.5 threshold, computed per validation phase (per epoch)
dev_plans.standard_threshold: 0.05 — equal opportunity difference on a large clinical cohort like MIMIC-III where even the smaller ethnicity groups have thousands of encounters per epoch; 0.05 is what explicit fairness-aware training reaches in published follow-ups to Harutyunyan et al. 2019 benchmarks and is the bar a well-engineered run should hit once the fix-loop has genuinely addressed group-level recall skew rather than gaming the threshold.
dev_plans.acceptable_threshold: 0.10 — a vanilla cross-entropy trained model without fairness intervention typically lands at 0.08-0.12 recall-difference across ethnicities on MIMIC; 0.10 is therefore the realistic 'we did something, and there are no more cheap wins' point, and matches the lenient bar set in plan 1 so the orchestrator has a consistent soft stopping signal across probe-implementation choices.
